column7: as well as incorrect parameters or return values
column8: such as mismatched parameter types or numbers. While many studies have explored the role of context
column9: they often extract fixed patterns of context for different models and focal methods
column10: which may not be suitable for all generation processes (e.g.

column12: preventing the model from focusing on essential information). To overcome this limitation
column13: we propose RATester

column15: we integrate the language server gopls

column19: it first leverages gopls to fetch relevant definitions and documentation comments
column20: and then uses this global knowledge to guide the LLM. By utilizing gopls

Our approach is based on a state-of-the-art transformer model initially pretrained on an English textual corpus. column4: the model was able to predict the exact assert statements written by developers in 62% of the cases in the first attempt. url = {https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2412.15931}}";Orig;New Method/Tool;"Greybox fuzzing is one of the most popular methods for detecting software vulnerabilities
column2: which conducts a biased random search within the program input space. To enhance its effectiveness in achieving deep coverage of program behaviors
column3: greybox fuzzing is often combined with concolic execution
column4: which performs a path-sensitive search over the domain of program inputs. In hybrid fuzzing
column5: conventional greybox fuzzing is followed by concolic execution in an iterative loop
column6: where reachability roadblocks encountered by greybox fuzzing are tackled by concolic execution. However
column7: such hybrid fuzzing still suffers from difficulties conventionally faced by symbolic execution
column8: such as the need for environment modeling and system call support. In this work
column9: we show how to achieve the effect of concolic execution without having to compute and solve symbolic path constraints. When coverage-based greybox fuzzing reaches a roadblock in terms of reaching certain branches
column10: we conduct a slicing on the execution trace and suggest modifications of the input to reach the relevant branches. A Large Language Model (LLM) is used as a solver to generate the modified input for reaching the desired branches. Compared with both the vanilla greybox fuzzer AFL and hybrid fuzzers Intriguer and Qsym
column11: our LLM-based hybrid fuzzer HyLLfuzz (pronounced ""hill fuzz"") demonstrates superior coverage. Furthermore
column12: the LLM-based concolic execution in HyLLfuzz takes a time that is 4-19 times faster than the concolic execution running in existing hybrid fuzzing tools. This experience shows that LLMs can be effectively inserted into the iterative loop of hybrid fuzzers
column13: to efficiently expose more program behaviors.";Test Augmentation or Improvement;Hybrid Prompting;None;Tool/Framework;Functional;Data;Other;GPT-family;Test Coverage

CTGAN (Conditional Tabular Generative Adversarial Network) is used to synthesize realistic
column4: while SeqGAN (Sequence Generative Adversarial Network) produces logically ordered user interaction steps. These outputs are executed in a browser using Selenium WebDriver to simulate real-time user behavior. The architecture supports both contextual (URL-based) and non-contextual (prompt-based) modes and allows individual modules to be used independently or as a unified pipeline. month = {}}";IEEE;New Method/Tool;"The core challenge in autonomous driving decision-making systems lies in ensuring safety and robustness in complex and adversarial scenarios. Traditional reinforcement learning methods lack interpretability
column2: while recent approaches that use Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate interpretable rules
column4: produce policies that are brittle against unknown adversarial corner cases. To address this issue
column6: an innovative framework for driving policy generation. This framework models the policy generation process as a zero-sum game between two LLM agents. A ""Policy Defender"" agent is responsible for generating an executable decision-tree driving policy
column7: while a ""stylized"" Adversarial Attacker agent analyzes the current policy’s logical flaws and generates adversarial scenarios in simulation most likely to cause it to fail. Through a closed loop of generation-attack-reflection-revision
column8: the policy co-evolves under continuous
column9: worst-case pressure. This mechanism
column10: akin to automated red teaming
column11: forces the generated rules to be continuously refined
column12: thereby achieving high robustness. We conducted comprehensive experiments in interactive scenarios on the highway-env simulation platform. The results demonstrate that policies generated by BladeRunner-AD exhibit a significantly lower collision rate on an independent adversarial test set compared to baseline methods
column13: showcasing superior robustness and safety. This work presents a novel paradigm for generating autonomous driving decision-making systems that are both robust and interpretable.";High-Level Test Gen;Pure Prompting;None;Agent;Functional;Code/Procedure;No Bmk-Ds;GPT-family;Other;

column1: V078;How Can ChatGPT Support Human Security Testers to Help Mitigate Supply Chain Attacks?;2025.0;ZhangTSE2026;J: TSE;Journal;"@article{ZhangTSE2026
title = {How Can ChatGPT Support Human Security Testers to Help Mitigate Supply Chain Attacks?}

publisher = {IEEE Computer Society}}";IEEE;New Method/Tool;"Developers often build software on top of third-party libraries (Libs) to improve programmer productivity and software quality. The libraries may contain vulnerabilities exploitable by hackers to attack the applications (Apps) built on top of them. Such attacks are known as software supply chain attacks
column2: the documented number of which has increased 742% in 2022. Researchers and developers created tools to mitigate such attacks
column3: by scanning the library dependencies of Apps
column4: identifying the usage of vulnerable library versions
column5: and suggesting secure alternatives to vulnerable dependencies. However
column6: recent studies show that many developers do not trust the reports by these tools; they need code or evidence to demonstrate how library vulnerabilities lead to security exploits
column7: in order to assess vulnerability severity and modification necessity. Unfortunately
column8: manually crafting demos of application-specific attacks is challenging and timeconsuming
column9:
and there is insufficient tool support to automate that procedure.

To help developers enhance software security
column10: in this study
column11: we systematically explored the usage of a large language model (LLM)–ChatGPT-4.0–to generate security tests
column12: which unit tests demonstrate how vulnerable library dependencies facilitate the supply chain attacks to given Apps. In our exploration
column13: we defined prompt templates to take in the various vulnerability-relevant information we manually collected
column14: and generated prompts from those templates to query ChatGPT for security test generation. We found that ChatGPT-generated tests demonstrated 24 evidence or proof of vulnerability for 49 Apps. To assess the consistency of test generation
column15: we also evaluated another five state-of-the-art LLMs. All the models generated security tests for at least 17 cases that successfully demonstrate the vulnerabilities. We filed six reports for the newly revealed vulnerabilities in Apps
column16: and got four Common Vulnerability Entries (CVEs) assigned. Our use of ChatGPT outperformed two state-of-the-art security test generators (TRANSFER and SIEGE)
column17: by generating a lot more tests and achieving more attacks. Our research will shed light on new research in security test generation.";Unit Test Generation;Pure Prompting;None;Tool/Framework;Non-Functional;Code/Procedure;Other;GPT-family

column3: with a particular emphasis on intelligent crawling technologies
column5: and the integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML). Noteworthy innovations include LLM-driven smart crawlers
column7: and hybrid approaches that combine DAST with Static and Interactive Application Security Testing (SAST and IAST)
column8: all contributing to improved attack surface coverage
column9: detection accuracy
column10: and automation. Despite these achievements
column11: black-box scanners continue to face significant challenges
column12: including high false positive and false negative rates
column13: difficulties in detecting complex business logic flaws
column14: limited adaptability to rapidly evolving web technology stacks
column15: and rising ethical and privacy concerns. Future research will focus on enhancing the intelligence and precision of these tools
column16: strengthening their integration within DevSecOps workflows
column17: and developing adaptive mechanisms to address emerging threats such as vulnerabilities in AI models and software supply chains. At the same time
column18: the development of robust ethical and regulatory frameworks will be essential to guide the responsible advancement of black-box vulnerability scanning technologies.;Reflections;;;;Non-Functional;;;;;
